original_reasoning: Arabic qasr = 'palace, castle, court' (derived via Aramaic from Latin castrum, same root as Caesar); Greek = 'Caesar'. Plausible Mediterranean etymological connection via the Roman loanword.
نبت=to grow/sprout; Ναβαταῖος=Nabataean (people name from Semitic root). Strong Semitic cognate via ethnonym
Arabic استرخى بطنه centers on slackening or giving way, closely matching Greek gave way or retreated.
Arabic خص has the core of assigning specially or making something one's own, close to Greek have/hold.
Arabic folding and Greek helix both preserve a turning, bending, or coiling conceptual core.
birdhaun=packhorse/mule vs bourdon=mule; same meaning, Arabic borrowed from Greek (via Byzantine)
Arabic gloss centers on continued blood flow beyond its normal days; Greek gloss centers on copious pouring. The chain through abundant flowing is clear.
Arabic أفازه = caused him to attain, and Greek = you have taken/received. This is a clear attainment/acquisition chain.
